case_metadata.case_name: Meridian Retail Group plc v TechFlow Solutions Limited
case_metadata.court: High Court of Justice, Business and Property Courts, TCC (KBD)

Meridian entered into the MSA in reliance upon the Representation. The Representation was false: the Platform was incapable of supporting that volume.

passages.chunk_text: The Claimant claims against the Defendant damages for breach of contract and/or negligence and/or misrepresentation arising out of the Defendant’s defective and delayed design, build and implementation of a cloud-based inventory and EPOS platform pursuant to a Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024, together with interest pursuant to section 35A of the Senior Courts Act 1981 and costs.

Statement of Truth: The Claimant believes that the facts stated in this Claim Form are true. The Claimant understands that proceedings for contempt of court may be brought against anyone who makes, or causes to be made, a false statement in a document verified by a statement of truth without an honest belief in its truth.
The Claimant ("Meridian") is a public limited company and operates a chain of approximately 180 retail stores in the United Kingdom together with an online sales channel.
The Defendant ("TechFlow") is a limited company which designs, supplies and implements retail software systems.

By a written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 ("the MSA"), TechFlow agreed to design, build, configure and implement for Meridian a cloud-based inventory management and electronic point-of-sale platform ("the Platform").
The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work ("the SOW") at Schedule 1, which set out the scope of the works, the implementation plan and the milestones.
The total charges payable under the MSA were £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against milestones.
It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that the go-live date for the Platform was fixed at 1 October 2024, and that time was of the essence in respect of that date.

At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024, prior to entering into the MSA, TechFlow by its Sales Director Mr Daniel Frost orally represented to Meridian that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions ("the Representation"). Meridian entered into the MSA in reliance upon the Representation. The Representation was false: the Platform was incapable of supporting that volume.
In breach of the MSA, TechFlow delivered the Platform late. The Platform did not go live until 18 November 2024, some seven weeks after the contractual go-live date.
Meridian did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope of the works. All delay was caused by TechFlow’s failure to allocate adequate and competent resources to the project.

On numerous occasions prior to go-live, Meridian warned TechFlow that the Platform was not ready and requested that go-live be deferred. TechFlow ignored those warnings and proceeded to go-live on 18 November 2024.

Following go-live, the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours during November and December 2024, causing widespread till failures and an inability to process sales across Meridian’s store estate.

The Platform contained numerous defects, including critical (Severity 1) failures in the stock-synchronisation module which caused stores to display and sell against incorrect stock figures. By reason of those defects the Platform was not of satisfactory quality and was not reasonably fit for its purpose.
Meridian did not accept the Platform or any part of it, and no acceptance or sign-off was given by Meridian at any time.
Further, TechFlow failed to provide adequate training to Meridian’s staff in the use of the Platform.
By reason of the matters aforesaid Meridian has suffered loss and damage. PARTICULARS:
Further, Meridian claims interest on such sums as may be found due, pursuant to section 35A of the Senior Courts Act 1981, at such rate and for such period as the Court thinks fit.

The Supplier shall design, build, configure, test and implement the cloud-based inventory management and EPOS platform ("the Platform") in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1 (the "SOW").
The Supplier shall perform the Services with reasonable skill and care and in accordance with good industry practice.
The total charges for the Services are £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against the milestones set out in the SOW and as may be varied by agreement in writing.

The Supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the target go-live date stated in the SOW. The parties acknowledge that the implementation timetable is a planning estimate and may be adjusted through the change control procedure.

Any change to the scope, deliverables or timetable shall be agreed in writing by both parties by means of a signed Change Order. A signed Change Order varies this Agreement to the extent stated in it.

Subject to clause 14.3, the Supplier shall not be liable to the Customer, whether in contract, tort (including negligence) or otherwise, for any loss of profit, loss of revenue, loss of anticipated savings, or any indirect or consequential loss.

Subject to clause 14.3, the Supplier’s total aggregate liability arising under or in connection with this Agreement shall not exceed the total charges actually paid by the Customer under this Agreement in the twelve months preceding the event giving rise to the claim.
Nothing in this Agreement limits liability for death or personal injury caused by negligence, for fraud, or for any other liability that cannot lawfully be excluded.
This Agreement, together with its Schedules, constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior representations, statements or agreements, whether written or oral.
Each party acknowledges that it has not relied on, and shall have no remedy in respect of, any representation or statement not expressly set out in this Agreement (save in the case of fraud).
This Agreement is governed by the law of England and Wales, and the parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales.
The Supplier shall deliver the Platform in two phases: Phase 1 (core inventory and EPOS); Phase 2 (reporting and analytics). The agreed scope does not include a customer loyalty module.
The go-live date of 1 October 2024 is a target date. The detailed implementation plan is a planning estimate and is subject to the change control procedure in clause 3 of the MSA.
The Customer shall provide timely access to data, systems and personnel, and shall procure and maintain adequate in-store network connectivity.

Training of the Customer’s store and head-office staff in the use of the Platform is the responsibility of the Customer. The Supplier shall provide a train-the-trainer session and written user guides only.
Charges are payable 25% on each of M1, M2, M3 and M4, subject to any variation agreed under the change control procedure.
Platform licence & implementation (Phase 1 — core inventory & EPOS) — Qty: 1 — Charge (£): 1,500,000
EPOS terminal configuration (180 stores) — Qty: 180 — Charge (£): 540,000
Reporting & analytics (Phase 2) — Qty: 1 — Charge (£): 360,000
With effect from the date of this Deed, clause 4.1 of the SOW is varied so that the charges are payable: 20% on M1, 20% on M2, 30% on M3 and 30% on M4.

Save as varied by this Deed, the MSA remains in full force and effect. EXECUTED as a DEED by the parties on the date first above written. For Meridian:	..........................  Priya Nair, Head of Procurement For TechFlow:	..........................  Gregory Manton, Commercial Director

At the Customer’s request, the Supplier shall design, build and implement a customer loyalty module ("the Loyalty Module"), comprising points accrual, redemption at EPOS, and a loyalty member database. The Loyalty Module was not part of the original scope under SOW-01.
The parties agree that, to accommodate the additional works, the go-live date is revised from 1 October 2024 to 18 November 2024. The revised date applies to the Platform as a whole.
The additional charge for the Loyalty Module is £180,000, payable on go-live. Agreed and signed by the parties:

This certificate confirms that User Acceptance Testing of Phase 1 of the Platform (core inventory and EPOS) was carried out between 21 October and 11 November 2024. Meridian confirms that Phase 1 has passed User Acceptance Testing and is accepted, subject to the resolution of the outstanding minor (Severity 3 and 4) items listed in the snag log appended to the UAT report. No outstanding Severity 1 or Severity 2 items were recorded at the date of this certificate.

Helena,
Following today’s readiness review, I want to put our recommendation in writing. The core build is in good shape and Phase 1 passed the bulk of UAT, but the loyalty module added under Change Order 3 is still being hardened and we have a handful of stock-sync issues we would like to close out under controlled conditions.
Our clear recommendation is to defer go-live to mid-January 2025, after the peak trading period. Going live on 18 November, immediately before Black Friday and Christmas, carries real risk and removes our contingency. We can hold the 18 November date if Meridian instructs us to, but we would be doing so against our advice.
Please let me know how you wish to proceed.
Marcus Reilly | Project Director | TechFlow Solutions

Marcus,
Thank you, but the board has been clear that we need the new platform live for peak trading — that is the whole point of doing this now. Missing Black Friday and Christmas is not acceptable commercially.
Please proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 as planned. I understand your team’s reservations and I am content to take that risk on Meridian’s side. Let’s get the remaining stock-sync items onto the snag list and press on.
Helena Vance | Programme Director | Meridian Retail Group

Marcus,
Following the marketing review, the board wants a customer loyalty capability included in the platform before we go live — points accrual, redemption at the till, and a member database. I appreciate this was not in the original SOW, but it is now a priority for us.
Can you scope this as a change and let us have the cost and any timetable impact? We accept there may be a knock-on to the go-live date and we are willing to discuss a revised date to accommodate it.
Helena

Helena,
Thanks. We’ve scoped the loyalty module. The additional cost is £180,000 and, given the additional build and test, we will need to move go-live from 1 October to 18 November 2024. I’ll issue Change Order 3 for Priya’s signature to confirm both the scope and the revised date.
Marcus

Helena, Raymond,
We’ve completed the incident review for yesterday’s outage (approx. 13:10–18:05, around five hours). The root cause was a loss of wide-area network connectivity at your data centre provided by Northgate Telecom, not the Platform. During the window the Platform application and database remained healthy but could not be reached because the network link was down.
We have confirmed this with Northgate’s incident report (ref NT-22841). We’d suggest raising it with them directly. Separately, we are continuing to work the stock-sync defects, which are a Platform issue and which we own.
Sanjay Patel | Service Manager | TechFlow Solutions

Helena,
A heads-up before the board call. December trading is well below plan and there are a few moving parts:
1) The Lutterworth distribution centre flooded on 3 December and was effectively out of action for around ten days. Roughly 40 stores had gaps on key lines through the first half of the month as a result. This is unrelated to the platform.
2) The market is soft — footfall is down across the sector and several competitors have gone early and deep on discounting. Our like-for-likes were already tracking behind in October.
3) On top of that, the platform stock-sync problems haven’t helped at the tills, though the teams have largely been working around them with manual checks.
I’d be wary of attributing the whole shortfall to the platform when the DC flood and the wider market are doing a lot of the damage. Happy to talk it through before the call.
Raymond
D-001 (19 Nov 2024, Sev 1): Stock-sync: tills show incorrect stock counts; oversell on promotional lines. Status: Platform defect — fixed 31 Dec 2024
D-002 (22 Nov 2024, Sev 1): Stock-sync: overnight reconciliation job fails intermittently. Status: Platform defect — fixed 2 Jan 2025
D-003 (25 Nov 2024, Sev 1): Total loss of EPOS connectivity, all stores, ~5 hours. Status: Network (Northgate Telecom) — not Platform
D-004 (29 Nov 2024, Sev 3): Receipt printer formatting incorrect on loyalty redemptions. Status: Platform defect — fixed 6 Dec 2024
D-005 (2 Dec 2024, Sev 4): Report export to CSV mis-orders columns. Status: Platform defect — fixed 9 Dec 2024
D-006 (11 Dec 2024, Sev 2): Intermittent till freezes traced to in-store broadband dropouts. Status: Store network — not Platform
Meridian Retail Group plc – Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 – NOTICE OF TERMINATION AND LETTER BEFORE ACTION
We act for Meridian Retail Group plc ("our client"). We write in connection with your design, build and implementation of the inventory and EPOS platform under the above Agreement ("the MSA").

Our client’s position is that you are in repudiatory breach of the MSA. In particular: (i) you delivered the Platform late; (ii) following go-live on 18 November 2024 the Platform suffered serious and repeated failures, including critical defects in the stock-synchronisation module; and (iii) the Platform was not of satisfactory quality nor fit for purpose. These failures occurred during our client’s most important trading period and caused substantial loss.

Our client accepts your conduct as terminating the MSA with immediate effect and reserves all of its rights. Our client will be seeking damages, including wasted expenditure and loss of profit during the peak trading period, which are substantial.
This letter is sent in accordance with the Practice Direction on Pre-Action Conduct. We require your substantive response within 14 days.
We act for TechFlow Solutions Limited. Your client’s claim is denied in its entirety. Liability and quantum are both disputed.

Timetable and scope. The go-live date was a target, not a fixed date. It moved from 1 October to 18 November 2024 by reason of Change Order No. 3, signed by your client on 2 September 2024, which added a customer loyalty module that your client requested and which was not in the original scope. Your client’s assertion that it requested no change to scope is wrong and is contradicted by its own signed Change Order.

Go-live decision. Our client expressly recommended, in writing on 24 October 2024, that go-live be deferred to January 2025 to avoid the peak trading period. Your client’s Programme Director rejected that recommendation and instructed our client to proceed on 18 November 2024, accepting the associated risk. Your client cannot now complain of the consequences of its own decision.

Availability. The Platform was not "unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours". The most significant outage, on 25 November 2024, was caused by the failure of your client’s own network provider, Northgate Telecom, and not by the Platform.

Loss. Your client’s pleaded loss of profit is grossly overstated and ignores (a) the flooding of your client’s Lutterworth distribution centre in December 2024 and (b) the well-documented downturn in the retail sector during that quarter. In any event, clause 14 of the MSA excludes liability for loss of profit and caps our client’s liability at the charges actually paid.
Outstanding sums. Your client has wrongly withheld the final two milestone payments totalling £600,000, which remain due to our client. Our client reserves the right to counterclaim.

I am Helena Vance, Programme Director at Meridian Retail Group plc. I make this statement from my own knowledge, save where I indicate otherwise. I had overall responsibility for the Platform implementation programme on Meridian’s side.
I was the main day-to-day contact with TechFlow. I worked closely with their Project Director, Marcus Reilly.

In around August 2024, our marketing team and the board decided we wanted a customer loyalty capability built into the new platform. I asked TechFlow to add a loyalty module. I accept this was not in the original Statement of Work. It was documented in Change Order No. 3, which my colleague Priya Nair signed on 2 September 2024, and which also moved the go-live date to 18 November 2024.

As we approached go-live, Marcus Reilly recommended in writing that we delay until January 2025, after the Christmas period. I decided that we should go live on 18 November 2024. The board was firm that we needed the platform live for peak trading, and I took the decision to proceed despite TechFlow’s advice. With hindsight, going live just before Black Friday was optimistic.

After go-live there were real problems with the stock-synchronisation module. Tills in some stores showed the wrong stock numbers, and on promotional lines we oversold in places. These problems were serious and took several weeks to bring under control. They caused a great deal of disruption for store teams during our busiest period.

There were also occasions when tills went down. I could not say for how long in total, and I understand some of the connectivity problems were to do with our own store broadband rather than the platform itself, but from where I sat it felt constant during the early weeks.
I considered that the platform we were given was not what we had paid for, and that is why Meridian decided to bring the relationship to an end.

I am Raymond Okafor, Head of Retail Operations at Meridian Retail Group plc. I make this statement from my own knowledge save where otherwise indicated. I am responsible for the running of our store estate.

The new platform went live on 18 November 2024, immediately before our peak trading period. The first few weeks were very difficult for store teams, particularly because of stock figures being wrong at the tills on the new system.

That said, I should be straight about the wider picture. A number of the till outages in stores turned out to be caused by the broadband connections in the stores dropping out, rather than the platform itself. There was also a complete outage on 25 November that I am told was a network failure at our data centre provider, not the platform.

Our trading in December 2024 was poor, but the platform was not the only reason. Our distribution centre at Lutterworth flooded on 3 December 2024 and was effectively out of action for about ten days, which left around 40 stores short of stock on key lines in the first half of December. The market was also weak that quarter, with footfall down across the sector and heavy discounting by competitors.

I raised these points internally at the time, including in an email to Helena Vance on 8 December 2024, because I was concerned we should not put the whole of a bad December down to the platform when there were other significant factors.
None of this is to say the platform problems did not hurt us — they did, especially the stock-sync issues — but they were one factor among several.

I am Priya Nair, Head of Procurement at Meridian Retail Group plc. I make this statement from my own knowledge. I was responsible for the contractual and commercial administration of the TechFlow contract.
I signed the Master Services Agreement on Meridian’s behalf on 14 March 2024, and the related Order Form. The total contract value was £2.4 million.

On 2 September 2024 I signed Change Order No. 3. This added the customer loyalty module, which Helena Vance had asked TechFlow to provide and which was not in the original Statement of Work, and it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024. I understood at the time that we were changing both the scope and the date.

On 12 November 2024 I signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate, confirming that Phase 1 of the platform had passed user acceptance testing. There were some minor snags listed, but no critical items were outstanding at that point.
In total Meridian paid TechFlow £1.8 million. We withheld the final two milestone payments of £300,000 each — £600,000 in total — because of the problems we experienced after go-live.
I am not in a position to comment on the technical performance of the platform; that is a matter for my operational and technical colleagues.

I am Dr Alan Whitfield, BSc PhD CEng FBCS. I have 28 years’ experience in the design and assurance of enterprise retail IT systems. I am instructed by Caldwell Pryce LLP, solicitors for the Claimant, to give my independent opinion on (a) the availability of the Platform following go-live and (b) whether the Platform contained defects falling below the standard reasonably to be expected.

I was provided with the Platform application and database logs, the network monitoring data and the defect log for the period 18 November 2024 to 10 January 2025. Having analysed the logs, my opinion is that the total Platform unavailability attributable to the Platform itself was approximately 6.2% of trading hours over the period.
That figure is materially lower than the figure of "more than 40% of trading hours" referred to in the pleadings. I have found no support in the logs for a figure of that order.

The single largest outage occurred on 25 November 2024 and lasted approximately five hours. In my opinion this outage was not caused by the Platform. The application and database remained healthy throughout; the cause was a loss of wide-area network connectivity at the Claimant’s data-centre provider (Northgate Telecom). A number of shorter in-store till interruptions were likewise attributable to in-store broadband, not to the Platform.

Notwithstanding the above, my opinion is that the Platform did contain genuine and serious defects in the stock-synchronisation module (defects D-001 and D-002 in the log). These were correctly categorised as Severity 1. They caused incorrect stock figures at the point of sale and overselling on certain lines, and persisted for approximately six weeks before being resolved.

In my opinion the presence of Severity 1 stock-synchronisation defects of this kind, persisting for around six weeks across a live store estate, falls below the standard reasonably to be expected of a competent supplier exercising reasonable skill and care.

In summary: (a) the Platform contained genuine and serious stock-synchronisation defects which fell below the expected standard; but (b) the pleaded allegation that the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours is not supported by the evidence, and the largest single outage was caused by the Claimant’s own network provider rather than the Platform.

I am Fiona Greenhalgh, a Fellow of the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales, and a partner in a forensic accounting practice. I am instructed by Caldwell Pryce LLP to give my independent opinion on the quantum of the Claimant’s pleaded losses, namely wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 and loss of profit of £4,200,000.

The pleaded wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is consistent with the Claimant’s accounting records and the sums invoiced and paid under the Master Services Agreement. I have no reason to challenge this element on a wasted-expenditure basis.

I have analysed the Claimant’s management accounts, EPOS sales data and prior-year comparatives for the November–December 2024 period. In my opinion the pleaded loss of profit of £4,200,000 is substantially overstated.
On the most supportable basis, the incremental lost gross profit capable of being attributed to the Platform’s defects is of the order of £1,300,000, not £4,200,000.

A material part of the Q4 sales shortfall is, in my opinion, attributable to causes unconnected with the Platform. In particular: (a) the flooding of the Claimant’s Lutterworth distribution centre in early December 2024, which disrupted stock to approximately 40 stores for around ten days; and (b) a sector-wide downturn in retail trading in the relevant quarter, evident in the Claimant’s own like-for-like data from October 2024 (i.e. before go-live). I estimate that approximately £1,500,000 of the claimed decline is attributable to these non-Platform factors, with the balance unsupported on the material I have seen.

I note, as a matter for the Court rather than for me, that clause 14 of the Master Services Agreement appears to exclude liability for loss of profit and to cap the Supplier’s liability at the charges paid (approximately £1,800,000). If that clause is effective, it would significantly affect the recoverability of the loss-of-profit claim regardless of my quantification.

In summary: the wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is supportable; the pleaded loss of profit of £4,200,000 is substantially overstated, with a supportable figure of around £1,300,000 before considering causation and the contractual limitation, and a significant part of the Q4 shortfall attributable to the distribution-centre flood and the wider market.

nodes.text: The Claimant ("Meridian") is a public limited company and operates a chain of approximately 180 retail stores in the United Kingdom together with an online sales channel.
The Defendant ("TechFlow") is a limited company which designs, supplies and implements retail software systems.

By a written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 ("the MSA"), TechFlow agreed to design, build, configure and implement for Meridian a cloud-based inventory management and electronic point-of-sale platform ("the Platform").
The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work ("the SOW") at Schedule 1, which set out the scope of the works, the implementation plan and the milestones.
The total charges payable under the MSA were £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against milestones.
It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that the go-live date for the Platform was fixed at 1 October 2024, and that time was of the essence in respect of that date.

At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024, prior to entering into the MSA, TechFlow by its Sales Director Mr Daniel Frost orally represented to Meridian that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions ("the Representation"). Meridian entered into the MSA in reliance upon the Representation. The Representation was false: the Platform was incapable of supporting that volume.
In breach of the MSA, TechFlow delivered the Platform late. The Platform did not go live until 18 November 2024, some seven weeks after the contractual go-live date.
Meridian did not at any time request any change to the agreed scope of the works. All delay was caused by TechFlow’s failure to allocate adequate and competent resources to the project.

On numerous occasions prior to go-live, Meridian warned TechFlow that the Platform was not ready and requested that go-live be deferred. TechFlow ignored those warnings and proceeded to go-live on 18 November 2024.

Following go-live, the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours during November and December 2024, causing widespread till failures and an inability to process sales across Meridian’s store estate.

The Platform contained numerous defects, including critical (Severity 1) failures in the stock-synchronisation module which caused stores to display and sell against incorrect stock figures. By reason of those defects the Platform was not of satisfactory quality and was not reasonably fit for its purpose.
Meridian did not accept the Platform or any part of it, and no acceptance or sign-off was given by Meridian at any time.
Further, TechFlow failed to provide adequate training to Meridian’s staff in the use of the Platform.

verbatim_texts: The Claimant claims against the Defendant damages for breach of contract and/or negligence and/or misrepresentation arising out of the Defendant’s defective and delayed design, build and implementation of a cloud-based inventory and EPOS platform pursuant to a Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024, together with interest pursuant to section 35A of the Senior Courts Act 1981 and costs.

By a written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 ("the MSA"), TechFlow agreed to design, build, configure and implement for Meridian a cloud-based inventory management and electronic point-of-sale platform ("the Platform").
The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work ("the SOW") at Schedule 1, which set out the scope of the works, the implementation plan and the milestones.
The total charges payable under the MSA were £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against milestones.
It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that the go-live date for the Platform was fixed at 1 October 2024, and that time was of the essence in respect of that date.

At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024, prior to entering into the MSA, TechFlow by its Sales Director Mr Daniel Frost orally represented to Meridian that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions ("the Representation").
Meridian entered into the MSA in reliance upon the Representation.
In breach of the MSA, TechFlow delivered the Platform late.

The Supplier shall design, build, configure, test and implement the cloud-based inventory management and EPOS platform ("the Platform") in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1 (the "SOW").
The total charges for the Services are £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against the milestones set out in the SOW and as may be varied by agreement in writing.
The Supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the target go-live date stated in the SOW.
The detailed implementation plan is a planning estimate and is subject to the change control procedure in clause 3 of the MSA.
Save as varied by this Deed, the MSA remains in full force and effect.
With effect from the date of this Deed, clause 4.1 of the SOW is varied so that the charges are payable: 20% on M1, 20% on M2, 30% on M3 and 30% on M4.
The Loyalty Module was not part of the original scope under SOW-01.

The core build is in good shape and Phase 1 passed the bulk of UAT, but the loyalty module added under Change Order 3 is still being hardened and we have a handful of stock-sync issues we would like to close out under controlled conditions.
I’ll issue Change Order 3 for Priya’s signature to confirm both the scope and the revised date.

nodes.label: Reilly to Vance, 24 Oct 2024
nodes.chunk_text:
Helena,
Following today’s readiness review, I want to put our recommendation in writing. The core build is in good shape and Phase 1 passed the bulk of UAT, but the loyalty module added under Change Order 3 is still being hardened and we have a handful of stock-sync issues we would like to close out under controlled conditions.
Our clear recommendation is to defer go-live to mid-January 2025, after the peak trading period. Going live on 18 November, immediately before Black Friday and Christmas, carries real risk and removes our contingency. We can hold the 18 November date if Meridian instructs us to, but we would be doing so against our advice.
Please let me know how you wish to proceed.
Marcus Reilly | Project Director | TechFlow Solutions

nodes.label: Vance to Reilly, 24 Oct 2024
nodes.chunk_text:
Marcus,
Thank you, but the board has been clear that we need the new platform live for peak trading — that is the whole point of doing this now. Missing Black Friday and Christmas is not acceptable commercially.
Please proceed to go-live on 18 November 2024 as planned. I understand your team’s reservations and I am content to take that risk on Meridian’s side. Let’s get the remaining stock-sync items onto the snag list and press on.
Helena Vance | Programme Director | Meridian Retail Group

nodes.label: Vance to Reilly, 21 Aug 2024
nodes.chunk_text:
Marcus,
Following the marketing review, the board wants a customer loyalty capability included in the platform before we go live — points accrual, redemption at the till, and a member database. I appreciate this was not in the original SOW, but it is now a priority for us.
Can you scope this as a change and let us have the cost and any timetable impact? We accept there may be a knock-on to the go-live date and we are willing to discuss a revised date to accommodate it.
Helena

nodes.label: Reilly to Vance, 27 Aug 2024
nodes.chunk_text:
Helena,
Thanks. We’ve scoped the loyalty module. The additional cost is £180,000 and, given the additional build and test, we will need to move go-live from 1 October to 18 November 2024. I’ll issue Change Order 3 for Priya’s signature to confirm both the scope and the revised date.
Marcus

nodes.chunk_text: Meridian Retail Group plc – Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 – NOTICE OF TERMINATION AND LETTER BEFORE ACTION

nodes.label: Caldwell Pryce LLP, 20 Jan 2025
nodes.chunk_text: We act for Meridian Retail Group plc ("our client"). We write in connection with your design, build and implementation of the inventory and EPOS platform under the above Agreement ("the MSA").

nodes.label: Caldwell Pryce LLP, 20 Jan 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Our client’s position is that you are in repudiatory breach of the MSA. In particular: (i) you delivered the Platform late; (ii) following go-live on 18 November 2024 the Platform suffered serious and repeated failures, including critical defects in the stock-synchronisation module; and (iii) the Platform was not of satisfactory quality nor fit for purpose. These failures occurred during our client’s most important trading period and caused substantial loss.

nodes.label: Harborne Quinn LLP, 7 Feb 2025
nodes.chunk_text: We act for TechFlow Solutions Limited. Your client’s claim is denied in its entirety. Liability and quantum are both disputed.

nodes.label: Harborne Quinn LLP, 7 Feb 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Timetable and scope. The go-live date was a target, not a fixed date. It moved from 1 October to 18 November 2024 by reason of Change Order No. 3, signed by your client on 2 September 2024, which added a customer loyalty module that your client requested and which was not in the original scope. Your client’s assertion that it requested no change to scope is wrong and is contradicted by its own signed Change Order.

nodes.label: Harborne Quinn LLP, 7 Feb 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Go-live decision. Our client expressly recommended, in writing on 24 October 2024, that go-live be deferred to January 2025 to avoid the peak trading period. Your client’s Programme Director rejected that recommendation and instructed our client to proceed on 18 November 2024, accepting the associated risk. Your client cannot now complain of the consequences of its own decision.

nodes.label: Harborne Quinn LLP, 7 Feb 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Availability. The Platform was not "unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours". The most significant outage, on 25 November 2024, was caused by the failure of your client’s own network provider, Northgate Telecom, and not by the Platform.

nodes.label: Harborne Quinn LLP, 7 Feb 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Loss. Your client’s pleaded loss of profit is grossly overstated and ignores (a) the flooding of your client’s Lutterworth distribution centre in December 2024 and (b) the well-documented downturn in the retail sector during that quarter. In any event, clause 14 of the MSA excludes liability for loss of profit and caps our client’s liability at the charges actually paid.

nodes.label: Harborne Quinn LLP, 7 Feb 2025
nodes.chunk_text: Outstanding sums. Your client has wrongly withheld the final two milestone payments totalling £600,000, which remain due to our client. Our client reserves the right to counterclaim.

nodes.label: Vance WS, para 1
nodes.chunk_text: I am Helena Vance, Programme Director at Meridian Retail Group plc. I make this statement from my own knowledge, save where I indicate otherwise. I had overall responsibility for the Platform implementation programme on Meridian’s side.

nodes.label: Vance WS, para 3
nodes.chunk_text: In around August 2024, our marketing team and the board decided we wanted a customer loyalty capability built into the new platform. I asked TechFlow to add a loyalty module. I accept this was not in the original Statement of Work. It was documented in Change Order No. 3, which my colleague Priya Nair signed on 2 September 2024, and which also moved the go-live date to 18 November 2024.

nodes.label: Vance WS, para 4
nodes.chunk_text: As we approached go-live, Marcus Reilly recommended in writing that we delay until January 2025, after the Christmas period. I decided that we should go live on 18 November 2024. The board was firm that we needed the platform live for peak trading, and I took the decision to proceed despite TechFlow’s advice. With hindsight, going live just before Black Friday was optimistic.

nodes.label: Vance WS, para 7
nodes.chunk_text: I considered that the platform we were given was not what we had paid for, and that is why Meridian decided to bring the relationship to an end.

nodes.label: Okafor WS, para 1
nodes.chunk_text: I am Raymond Okafor, Head of Retail Operations at Meridian Retail Group plc. I make this statement from my own knowledge save where otherwise indicated. I am responsible for the running of our store estate.

nodes.label: Okafor WS, para 2
nodes.chunk_text: The new platform went live on 18 November 2024, immediately before our peak trading period. The first few weeks were very difficult for store teams, particularly because of stock figures being wrong at the tills on the new system.

nodes.label: Okafor WS, para 4
nodes.chunk_text: Our trading in December 2024 was poor, but the platform was not the only reason. Our distribution centre at Lutterworth flooded on 3 December 2024 and was effectively out of action for about ten days, which left around 40 stores short of stock on key lines in the first half of December. The market was also weak that quarter, with footfall down across the sector and heavy discounting by competitors.

nodes.label: Nair WS, para 2
nodes.chunk_text: I signed the Master Services Agreement on Meridian’s behalf on 14 March 2024, and the related Order Form. The total contract value was £2.4 million.

nodes.label: Nair WS, para 3
nodes.chunk_text: On 2 September 2024 I signed Change Order No. 3. This added the customer loyalty module, which Helena Vance had asked TechFlow to provide and which was not in the original Statement of Work, and it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024. I understood at the time that we were changing both the scope and the date.

nodes.label: Nair WS, para 4
nodes.chunk_text: On 12 November 2024 I signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate, confirming that Phase 1 of the platform had passed user acceptance testing. There were some minor snags listed, but no critical items were outstanding at that point.

nodes.label: Nair WS, para 5
nodes.chunk_text: In total Meridian paid TechFlow £1.8 million. We withheld the final two milestone payments of £300,000 each — £600,000 in total — because of the problems we experienced after go-live.

nodes.label: Whitfield Expert Report, para 2
nodes.chunk_text: I was provided with the Platform application and database logs, the network monitoring data and the defect log for the period 18 November 2024 to 10 January 2025. Having analysed the logs, my opinion is that the total Platform unavailability attributable to the Platform itself was approximately 6.2% of trading hours over the period.

nodes.label: Whitfield Expert Report, para 3
nodes.chunk_text: That figure is materially lower than the figure of "more than 40% of trading hours" referred to in the pleadings. I have found no support in the logs for a figure of that order.

nodes.label: Whitfield Expert Report, para 5
nodes.chunk_text: Notwithstanding the above, my opinion is that the Platform did contain genuine and serious defects in the stock-synchronisation module (defects D-001 and D-002 in the log). These were correctly categorised as Severity 1. They caused incorrect stock figures at the point of sale and overselling on certain lines, and persisted for approximately six weeks before being resolved.

nodes.label: Whitfield Expert Report, para 7
nodes.chunk_text: In summary: (a) the Platform contained genuine and serious stock-synchronisation defects which fell below the expected standard; but (b) the pleaded allegation that the Platform was unavailable for more than 40% of trading hours is not supported by the evidence, and the largest single outage was caused by the Claimant’s own network provider rather than the Platform.

nodes.label: Greenhalgh Expert Report, para 2
nodes.chunk_text: The pleaded wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is consistent with the Claimant’s accounting records and the sums invoiced and paid under the Master Services Agreement. I have no reason to challenge this element on a wasted-expenditure basis.

nodes.label: Greenhalgh Expert Report, para 6
nodes.chunk_text: I note, as a matter for the Court rather than for me, that clause 14 of the Master Services Agreement appears to exclude liability for loss of profit and to cap the Supplier’s liability at the charges paid (approximately £1,800,000). If that clause is effective, it would significantly affect the recoverability of the loss-of-profit claim regardless of my quantification.

verbatim_texts: The Claimant claims against the Defendant damages for breach of contract and/or negligence and/or misrepresentation arising out of the Defendant’s defective and delayed design, build and implementation of a cloud-based inventory and EPOS platform pursuant to a Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024, together with interest pursuant to section 35A of the Senior Courts Act 1981 and costs.

By a written Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 ("the MSA"), TechFlow agreed to design, build, configure and implement for Meridian a cloud-based inventory management and electronic point-of-sale platform ("the Platform").
The MSA incorporated a Statement of Work ("the SOW") at Schedule 1, which set out the scope of the works, the implementation plan and the milestones.
The total charges payable under the MSA were £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against milestones.
It was an express term of the MSA and the SOW that the go-live date for the Platform was fixed at 1 October 2024, and that time was of the essence in respect of that date.

At a meeting on or about 5 February 2024, prior to entering into the MSA, TechFlow by its Sales Director Mr Daniel Frost orally represented to Meridian that the Platform would reliably support at least 10,000 concurrent transactions ("the Representation").
Meridian entered into the MSA in reliance upon the Representation.
In breach of the MSA, TechFlow delivered the Platform late.

The Supplier shall design, build, configure, test and implement the cloud-based inventory management and EPOS platform ("the Platform") in accordance with the Statement of Work set out at Schedule 1 (the "SOW").
The total charges for the Services are £2,400,000 (excluding VAT), payable against the milestones set out in the SOW and as may be varied by agreement in writing.
The Supplier shall use reasonable endeavours to achieve the target go-live date stated in the SOW.
The detailed implementation plan is a planning estimate and is subject to the change control procedure in clause 3 of the MSA.
Save as varied by this Deed, the MSA remains in full force and effect.
With effect from the date of this Deed, clause 4.1 of the SOW is varied so that the charges are payable: 20% on M1, 20% on M2, 30% on M3 and 30% on M4.
The Loyalty Module was not part of the original scope under SOW-01.

The core build is in good shape and Phase 1 passed the bulk of UAT, but the loyalty module added under Change Order 3 is still being hardened and we have a handful of stock-sync issues we would like to close out under controlled conditions.
I’ll issue Change Order 3 for Priya’s signature to confirm both the scope and the revised date.
Status: Network (Northgate Telecom) — not Platform
Meridian Retail Group plc – Master Services Agreement dated 14 March 2024 – NOTICE OF TERMINATION AND LETTER BEFORE ACTION
We write in connection with your design, build and implementation of the inventory and EPOS platform under the above Agreement ("the MSA").
Our client’s position is that you are in repudiatory breach of the MSA.
Our client accepts your conduct as terminating the MSA with immediate effect and reserves all of its rights.
In any event, clause 14 of the MSA excludes liability for loss of profit and caps our client’s liability at the charges actually paid.
Go-live decision.
The most significant outage, on 25 November 2024, was caused by the failure of your client’s own network provider, Northgate Telecom, and not by the Platform.
I accept this was not in the original Statement of Work.
I signed the Master Services Agreement on Meridian’s behalf on 14 March 2024, and the related Order Form.

This added the customer loyalty module, which Helena Vance had asked TechFlow to provide and which was not in the original Statement of Work, and it revised the go-live date from 1 October to 18 November 2024.
On 12 November 2024 I signed the Phase 1 Acceptance Certificate, confirming that Phase 1 of the platform had passed user acceptance testing.
The application and database remained healthy throughout; the cause was a loss of wide-area network connectivity at the Claimant’s data-centre provider (Northgate Telecom).
I was provided with the Platform application and database logs, the network monitoring data and the defect log for the period 18 November 2024 to 10 January 2025.
Notwithstanding the above, my opinion is that the Platform did contain genuine and serious defects in the stock-synchronisation module (defects D-001 and D-002 in the log).
The pleaded wasted expenditure of £1,800,000 is consistent with the Claimant’s accounting records and the sums invoiced and paid under the Master Services Agreement.

I note, as a matter for the Court rather than for me, that clause 14 of the Master Services Agreement appears to exclude liability for loss of profit and to cap the Supplier’s liability at the charges paid (approximately £1,800,000).
